title: Timescales for Deep and Full Thermalization
authors: Tabea Herrmann, Felix Fritzsch, Arnd Bäcker
abstract: Isolated quantum systems typically approach thermal equilibrium as described by the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH). Going beyond this involves either higher order correlators (full thermalization) or the formation of state designs, i.e., the approach of moments of state ensembles after a projective measurement towards thermal equilibrium (deep thermalization). We compare these two extensions of ETH using extensive numerical studies within a paradigmatic model for chaotic many-body quantum dynamics. For this we find exponential relaxation for both extensions: For deep thermalization all moments relax with the same rate, which approximately equals the relaxation rate of the autocorrelation function captured by ETH. In contrast, higher order correlation functions in full thermalization approach equilibrium faster. This means that at higher orders full thermalization is faster than deep thermalization.
summary: The paper investigates whether higher-order correlations or the approach to state designs happens faster in chaotic quantum systems. It finds that while both processes are exponential, full thermalization (decay of OTOCs) accelerates with higher orders, making it faster than deep thermalization.
main_problem: Comparing the relaxation timescales of two extensions of the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH): full thermalization (higher-order correlation functions) and deep thermalization (approach of projected ensembles to Haar-random states).
main_result: Both processes exhibit exponential relaxation, but full thermalization is faster than deep thermalization at higher orders, as higher-order OTOCs decay at increasing rates while deep thermalization rates remain roughly constant.
method: Large-scale numerical simulations of a kicked Ising chain combined with theoretical frameworks including Free Probability Theory, Random Matrix Theory, and the replica picture.
model_or_system: A 1D kicked mixed-field Ising spin chain subject to periodic (Floquet) dynamics with random longitudinal fields, serving as a paradigme for chaotic many-body quantum dynamics.
key_observables: Higher-order Out-of-Time-Order Correlators (OTOCs) for full thermalization and moments of the projected ensemble for deep thermalization.
important_parameters: Order of the correlation function (k), subsystem size (LA), system size (L), and the chaotic/ergodic regime.
main_assumptions: The system is in a chaotic regime where it acts as its own bath; calculations often utilize the infinite temperature state or pure states.
figures_summary: Figures show the time dependence of OTOCs and ensemble moments exhibiting exponential decay and saturation at plateaus; they also illustrate the even-odd effect in deep thermalization and the scaling of fluctuations with Hilbert space dimension.
paper_structure: The paper defines the mathematical distinction between full and deep thermalization, proposes a unified comparison framework via k-replica observables, uses the kicked Ising chain as a case study to compare decay rates, and employs Random Matrix Theory to explain late-time scaling and fluctuations.
why_interesting: It provides a new quantitative comparison between two fundamental ways many-body systems reach equilibrium, offering insights into the hierarchy of timescales for information scrambling and state design formation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Time dependence of full thermalization Fk, Eq. (10), and deep thermalization ∆k, Eq. (13), in the kicked field Ising model averaged over 150 spin chain realizations. The numerical data for one Gell-Mann matrix is shown for L = 12, 16, 20 (light to dark colors) and LA = 2. Bold lines show the exponential behavior found by a median regression in the corresponding time interval for all Gell-Mann matrices.
FIG. 2. Saturation values of (a) full thermalization F sat

FIG. 3. Time dependence of (a) full thermalization Fk, Eq. (10), and (b) deep thermalization ∆k, Eq. (13), in the kicked field Ising model averaged over 150 spin chain realiza- tions. The numerical data for the 15 non-trivial Gell-Mann matrices corresponding to LA = 2 is shown for L = 20 and k = 1. Bold lines show the exponential behavior found by a median regression in the corresponding time interval for all non-trivial Gell-Mann matrices.

FIG. 5. Thermalization rates of (a) full thermalization γF and (b) deep thermalization γ∆. Similar to Fig. 4 but here for LA = 1, i.e. Pauli matrices, for L = 8, . . . , 18 (150 spin chain realizations) and L = 20 (100 realizations). Data is shown for k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

FIG. 6. Thermalization rates of (a) full thermalization γF and (b) deep thermalization γ∆for Gell-Mann matrices with LA = 2 (red plus) and Pauli matrices LA = 1 (blue cross). For the case of deep thermalization we also show for even k and LA = 2 results of mean regression in early time interval t ∈[2, 7] (pink square). For k = 1, 2, 3, 4 we show L = 20 for k = 5, 6 L = 18.

FIG. 7. Time dependence of deep thermalization ∆k, Eq. (13), in the kicked field Ising model. Numerical data for k = 2 and L = 20 and (a) for the three Pauli matrices for LA = 1 (averaged over 150 chain realizations), (b) for the 15 Gell-Mann matrices for LA = 2 (100 realizations), and (c) for 15 Pauli strings for LA = 2 (100 realizations). Bold lines show the exponential behavior found by a median regression in the corresponding time interval.

FIG. 8. Time dependence of deep thermalization in terms of ∆k and δk in the kicked field Ising model, averaged over 100 chain realizations. Data is shown for k = 1 (black circle and light blue square) and k = 2 (dark pink plus and dark blue cross) for L = 20 and LA = 2. Solid lines show exponential decay with thermalization rates γ∆1 and γ∆2. Note that for ∆k only ∆k for a single Gell-Mann matrix is visualized.

FIG. 9. Time dependence of full thermalization in terms of Fk in the x−polarized state |ψ⟩(black circle and dark pink plus), F inf k in the infinite temperature state (blue square and dark blue cross) and avenged Fk for three orthogonal states |ψj⟩(red triangle and orange star) in the kicked field Ising model, averaged over 150 chain realizations. Data of a single Gell-Mann matrix is shown for k = 1 and k = 2 for L = 12, LA = 2.

FIG. 10. Time dependence of deep thermalization in terms of ∆k and δk in the kicked field Ising model, averaged over 100 chain realizations. Data is shown for k = 1 (red circle and light blue square) and k = 2 (orange plus and dark blue cross) for L = 20 and LA = 2. For ∆k the average over 100 GUE matrices is shown.
